artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Thompson <dan.thompson86.mn@gmail.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-03-09 21:14
Subject: Money / condo / future brain dump

Hey Rach,

You’re probably already crashed for your early shift, but I wanted to write this out while it’s still swirling in my head instead of just staring at the ceiling at Minnehaha again.

I heard back from Twin Cities Federal Credit Union on Friday about the pre-approval. Brian Keller (the loan officer on Lake Street) finally sent the numbers and… they’re lower than I was hoping. He broke it down in this email titled “Re: Pre-Approval Limits for Condo Purchase – Daniel Thompson.” Basically they’re looking at the roughly $18k I still owe on my U of M loans plus about $4,500 in credit card balances from the 2020–2021 pandemic months, and that’s capping what they’ll give us.

I forwarded you his message earlier, but the short version is: the condo budget we’d been daydreaming about in Longfellow / Seward might have to shrink unless we wait longer or I kill off more of that debt first. I feel weirdly embarrassed about it, like I should have had this cleaned up by 39.

It’s also hard not to connect it to the health stuff. Ever since that October 2024 appointment with Dr. Karen Liu at the M Health Fairview Riverside clinic, when she said my LDL was high enough that she wanted me on a low-dose statin, I’ve had this constant background hum of “you’re behind” in my head. I told her I wanted to try six months of stricter food and exercise instead, hence the ridiculous spreadsheet on my laptop named “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?”

So now I’ve got this triangle of things stressing me out: the debt, the cholesterol, and trying to be the guy who can actually afford the kind of place you deserve to study in and sleep in without being woken up by my on‑call alerts.

I know you keep saying we’re a team and that we don’t have to rush the move next summer if it doesn’t make sense. I believe you, but I still want to pull my weight. Maybe we sit down next Sunday after coffee and actually run the numbers together? We can look at Brian’s email, my “Cholesterol Plan” sheet, and your post‑bacc tuition stuff side by side and see what a realistic timeline looks like instead of just guessing.

Sorry this is a lot. I just didn’t want to dump it on you in a 30‑second goodnight call.

Love you,
D

From: Daniel Thompson <dan.thompson86.mn@gmail.com>
To: Laura Jensen <laura.jensen@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-11-18 22:02
Subject: Dad / Southdale / next steps

Hey Laur,

Just got back to the apartment and wanted to write while everything from today at Southdale is still clear in my head.

Hearing the neurologist at M Health Fairview Southdale in Edina actually say “early-stage vascular dementia” about Dad made it all feel very real, even though we’ve both seen the signs for months. The way he kept losing track of the conversation when we were going over the car insurance was basically the same thing the doctor described.

On the practical side, I’ve already started quietly taking over some of the online stuff for them from here on Minnehaha. I set up a shared Google Sheet called “Thompson Household – Do Not Delete” (dramatic, I know) where I’m listing out their utilities, the Southdale bills, and the recurring charges so nothing slips. I logged into their checking account tonight to double-check the auto-pays. I’ll send you view access so you can see what’s going on and add anything I’ve missed.

We should probably talk about power of attorney sooner rather than later. Mom looked wiped when I left, and I don’t think she fully grasps how much work this is going to be if we don’t get organized now.

All of this is landing on top of the work and health stuff and I can feel it in my body. That October 2024 appointment with Dr. Liu at the Riverside clinic is still echoing in my head—her saying my LDL is high enough that she wants me on statins, me bargaining for six months of diet and exercise instead. I’ve been tracking everything in that “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?” spreadsheet like a nerd, but weeks like this don’t exactly help the whole “less stress” part.

I also have my next session with Dr. Emily Hart at the Walk-In Counseling Center on East Franklin later this week. In my calendar it just shows up as “E. Hart – stress appt.” I’ll definitely bring up Dad’s diagnosis and the money stuff, because between on‑call anxiety about screwing up something at North Star and now managing their bills, my brain is doing cartwheels.

Let’s do a video call Wednesday after the kids are down? I can walk you through the sheet and we can figure out how to talk to Mom about driving, etc.

Love you,
Dan

Title: Sunday night brain dump – work / health / school
Date: 2025-01-26 20:47
Location: 2841 Minnehaha – living room

- Work
  - Still thinking about the August 14, 2025 firewall incident at the Bloomington data center. Seven minutes of outpatient clinic traffic getting blocked because I overrode that change-control step on the rule push for the 2026 replacement project. Elena logged it in the change system as “Firewall Rule Push – 08/14/25 Near Miss – Daniel T.” and gave me the “informal warning” speech about future project lead eligibility.
  - Ever since, every time I’m in a maintenance window I stare at the little compass tattoo on my left wrist and try to slow my breathing. Feels like I’m one fat‑fingered command away from proving my anxiety right.

- Health
  - October 2024: Dr. Liu at M Health Fairview Clinic – Riverside tells me LDL is high enough to start a low-dose statin. I push back, ask for six months to try diet + exercise first. She agrees, but I can tell she thinks I’ll be back on meds.
  - Spreadsheet on laptop literally titled “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?” is actually helping. Weight hovering ~188, biking West River Parkway when weather cooperates, more stir‑fries, less late‑night delivery.

- Mental
  - Ongoing sessions with Dr. Emily Hart at the Walk-In Counseling Center on East Franklin (calendar shorthand: “E. Hart – stress appt.”) have been a lifesaver. Talked last time about the on‑call anxiety—fear of making a change that impacts patient care at North Star—and how it tied into the firewall near miss.
  - Also need to bring up Dad’s memory stuff and Mom’s comment about Rachel being “too focused on her career and not really family‑minded” that I overheard in their Bloomington kitchen back in May 2023. Still sitting in the back of my head whenever Rachel and I talk about moving in together or kids.

- School / career
  - January 2024: quietly enrolled in that self‑paced grad‑level networking course through UMN’s College of Continuing & Professional Studies. Paid out of pocket after North Star said it wasn’t on the approved list for reimbursement.
  - Worried that if I flame out on it, it means I’m not cut out for a Network Architect role, but also trying to remember the compass tattoo is about making deliberate choices, not perfection.

metadata.title: Sunday night brain dump – work / health / school

artifacts.content:
Title: E. Hart – stress appt.
Date: 2025-03-20 16:00–17:00
Location: Walk-In Counseling Center, East Franklin Ave, Minneapolis
Attendees: Daniel Thompson, Dr. Emily Hart

Notes / prep:

- Work stress
  - Talk about how the August 14, 2025 firewall “near miss” at the Bloomington data center is still living rent‑free in my brain. Elena’s incident note (“Firewall Rule Push – 08/14/25 Near Miss – Daniel T.”) pops into my head every time I open the change management system.
  - On‑call dread: fear of making a critical mistake that affects patient care at North Star Health Network. How I compulsively re‑check firewall rules and VPN configs at 2 a.m.

- Boundaries / ethics
  - Confess (again) that I’ve been using my admin access to peek at Rachel’s shift assignments in the internal scheduling system since late 2022. First time was that December 2022 night she got floated from pediatrics to the COVID overflow unit at the Saint Paul campus and texted me in a panic.
  - I justified it as “I just want to time coffee drop‑offs and rides,” but I know it’s a policy gray area at best and a HIPAA/IT violation at worst. Guilt + fear about what happens if an audit ever flags my logins.

- Health anxiety
  - Follow‑up on the October 2024 M Health Fairview Clinic – Riverside visit with Dr. Liu. She recommended starting a low‑dose statin because my LDL was high; I declined meds and promised six months of strict diet and exercise instead.
  - Mention the “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?” spreadsheet and how it’s both motivating and a constant reminder that my body isn’t invincible.

- Family
  - Dad’s early-stage vascular dementia diagnosis at M Health Fairview Southdale in November 2024. Managing their bills from my Minnehaha apartment via the “Thompson Household – Do Not Delete” Google Sheet.
  - Worry about burnout from juggling work, my UMN grad‑level networking course, and family care.

metadata.title: E. Hart – stress appt.
metadata.location: Walk-In Counseling Center – East Franklin Ave

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Thompson <dan.thompson86.mn@gmail.com>
To: Michael & Ellen Thompson <michael.ellen.thompson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-05 19:36
Subject: Online bills + little updates

Hi Mom and Dad,

I just finished going through the online accounts from here at the apartment, so I wanted to send a quick summary while it’s fresh.

For the “Thompson Household – Do Not Delete” spreadsheet I set up, I added:
- Xcel Energy – auto‑pay is on and the January bill is already scheduled.
- CenterPoint – paid the December balance manually, now set to auto‑pay on the 15th.
- M Health Fairview Southdale – I put in the account number for the neurology clinic so we can track the invoices related to Dad’s early-stage vascular dementia workup.

Dad, I know that phrase still sounds scary. The doctor at Southdale emphasized that it’s early and that staying on top of meds, walks, and routines can really help. This is just me trying to make the “paperwork” part less stressful for both of you.

Mom, you mentioned having trouble remembering which password goes with which site. As long as you call or text me when something new comes in the mail, I can log in from Minnehaha and take care of it. We’ll keep you in the loop on everything in the spreadsheet so you don’t feel like it’s disappearing into a black box.

On my end, I’m trying to practice what I preach about health. When I saw Dr. Liu at the M Health Fairview Riverside clinic back in October, she said my LDL cholesterol was high enough that she wanted me on a low-dose statin. I asked if I could do six months of stricter diet and more exercise first, and she agreed, so now I’ve got this nerdy “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?” file where I’m tracking bike rides and what I eat.

Between that, work at North Star, and the grad‑level networking class I started through UMN’s continuing studies last January, I’m learning that I can’t just “wing it” anymore. Systems and routines, right?

I’ll be over on Sunday like usual to help with the printer and shovel if it snows.

Love,
Dan

Thread: Dan & College Friend (Evan)
Date: 2025-02-27 evening

Evan: You still on for trivia tonight? Team “Packet Sniffers” needs its captain.

Dan: Might have to bail, man. Got my “E. Hart – stress appt.” at 4 over on East Franklin and I’m usually pretty wiped after talking about my neuroses for an hour.

Evan: Still seeing that counselor?

Dan: Yeah, Dr. Emily Hart at the Walk-In Counseling Center. Started in early 2023 when on‑call anxiety got bad. Keeps me from spiraling about taking down half of North Star with a typo.

Evan: lol dark but fair. That because of that firewall thing you mentioned?

Dan: Partly. Remember the August 14, 2025 maintenance window at the Bloomington data center I told you about? I overrode a change‑control step on the firewall rule push for the 2026 project, blocked outpatient clinic access for 7 minutes. Elena logged it as “Firewall Rule Push – 08/14/25 Near Miss – Daniel T.” and gave me the “another one of these and no more leading projects” talk.

Evan: Ouch. That would live in my head rent‑free too.

Dan: It does. Combine that with the October 2024 labs where Dr. Liu at Riverside said my LDL was high enough that she wanted me on statins… my body basically hums.

Evan: You on the meds now?

Dan: Not yet. I bargained for six months of strict diet/exercise instead. Got a spreadsheet literally named “Cholesterol Plan – Do I Really Need Statins?” because I’m still me.

Evan: Nerd. Proud of you though.

Dan: Add in Dad’s vascular dementia diagnosis at Southdale in November and me running their bills from my Minnehaha place via a sheet called “Thompson Household – Do Not Delete,” plus quietly paying for that UMN grad‑level networking class out of pocket after work wouldn’t reimburse… trivia might be a bridge too far tonight.

Evan: Fair. Rain check. If you change your mind, we’re at Moon Palace at 7.

Dan: Appreciate it. I’ll aim for next week when my brain isn’t a full stack trace of anxiety.

From: Daniel Thompson <dan.thompson86.mn@gmail.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-06-23 23:01
Subject: Random late-night honesty dump

Hey love,

You’re asleep next to me and I’m typing this on dim mode because my brain won’t shut up. I’ll probably just hand you my phone in the morning.

I keep thinking about that Sunday at my parents’ place in Bloomington in May 2023, when I was grabbing plates from the dining room and overheard Mom telling Laura in the kitchen that she worries you’re “too focused on your career and not really family‑minded.” It’s been stuck in my head ever since.

Every time we talk about moving in together next summer or the idea of kids after you finish the post‑bacc at St. Kate’s, that sentence pops up like some stupid error dialog. I hate that it’s coloring how I imagine introducing real plans to them, because it’s not fair to you and it’s not even based on who you actually are.

Layered on top of that is the whole “am I good enough at my job to keep up with you?” thing. I quietly enrolled in that self‑paced grad‑level networking course through UMN’s College of Continuing & Professional Studies back in January 2024, paid for it myself after North Star said it wasn’t on their approved list. Part of me is excited—I like the material—but part of me is terrified that if I stall out or bomb the final, it proves I’m not cut out for the Network Architect path I talk about.

Then there’s the gray‑area stuff I haven’t really owned up to. Since late 2022 I’ve been occasionally using my admin access at North Star to peek at your shift assignments in the internal scheduling system. The first time was that night in December 2022 when you got floated from pediatrics to the COVID overflow unit at the Saint Paul campus and texted me in full panic mode. I logged in, checked your unit and end time so I could plan a coffee drop‑off and be there when you got off.

It felt sweet and protective in the moment, but the more I sit with it, the more it feels like a boundary I shouldn’t be crossing, even with good intentions. If an audit ever pulled my access logs, it would look bad for both of us.

I’m not telling you this to freak you out, just because I don’t want there to be any secret corners between us. You deserve the full, messy version of me—family baggage, career insecurity, bad ethical calls and all.

We can talk about it when you’re awake and caffeinated.

Love,
D

Hey Laura,

I hope everything's going well with you, Greg, and the kids! I wanted to fill you in on some of my recent updates.

Life's been a bit of a whirlwind lately, but in a good way. Still settled in my apartment on Minnehaha Avenue—can you believe it's been since June 2022? Minneapolis has been great, especially being closer to you all and my favorite spots. Sophie and Oliver must have grown so much since the last time I saw them!

Work at North Star Health Network is keeping me busy as always. We're gearing up for a big firewall project that's set to launch in early 2026. It's a bit stressful, coordinating with all the hospital IT teams, but I enjoy the challenge. On top of that, I'm trying to stay on top of my health. I had a checkup recently and got a heads up about my cholesterol, so I've been making some changes—less late-night snacks and more bike rides when I can.

Rachel and I have been discussing moving in together next summer. She's wrapping up her post-bacc program, and we’re looking for a place around here with a bit more space. Something with in-unit laundry would be perfect, and maybe a room she can use for studying. Balancing everything isn't easy, but it's exciting to think about our future together.

Also, I've been weighing the possibility of buying a condo in the next few years. I've been running the numbers, but with the rising rents, it's still a bit up in the air. Perhaps it'll all line up soon. 

By the way, I'll be visiting Mom and Dad in Bloomington this weekend. I promised to help Dad with some tech issues, and you know how he gets when the Wi-Fi acts up! If you're free, maybe we can all grab lunch or something. Let me know if Sunday works for you.

Catch you soon!

Love,

Daniel R. Thompson

P.S. Just realized I never told you about the compass tattoo I got in my late twenties. It's a small reminder to keep making deliberate choices about where I'm headed, both literally and figuratively. Oh, and I think I still have that photo from Sophie's soccer game last year if you want it!

---
